title: Detect stalled agents: running but no token progress
description:
Detect stalled agents using OpenCode session infrastructure instead of token count polling.

CONTEXT: Agents can get stuck in "running" state. Current detection requires manual orch status comparison. But OpenCode already has the signals we need.

BETTER APPROACH (from opencode-fork and agent-lifecycle models):

1. GET /session/status already exists — returns idle/busy/retry per session
2. SSE event stream already flows to daemon — session.status, message.part.updated events
3. Session metadata API (implemented Feb 14) can store beads_id at spawn time

STALL DETECTION:
- Session status = "busy" (model claims to be working)
- No SSE events (message.part.updated, part.updated) for >3 minutes
- This means "busy but silent" = stalled
- Distinct from "idle" (model finished) and "busy with output" (model working)

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. In daemon SSE listener, track last-event-time per session
2. If session is busy and last-event-time > 3 minutes ago, flag as STALLED
3. Surface in dashboard as distinct state (not idle, not busy — stalled)
4. Surface in orch status: "⚠️ STALLED (busy but no output for 3m)"
5. Store beads_id in session metadata at spawn time for direct correlation

DO NOT: Poll token counts via orch status comparison (hacky, unreliable)
USE: OpenCode native signals (session.status + SSE event recency)

REFERENCES:
- .kb/models/opencode-fork.md — GET /session/status, session metadata API, SSE events
- .kb/models/agent-lifecycle-state-model.md — Failure Mode 3: Agent Went Idle But Not Complete
- .kb/models/opencode-session-lifecycle.md — SSE event structure, completion detection

VERIFICATION:
- Agent that stalls (no output for 3m while busy) gets flagged in dashboard
- orch status shows STALLED state
- No false positives on agents doing long tool calls (tool calls still emit SSE events)
- Stall detection uses OpenCode SSE events, not token count polling

description: Three agents spawned for the same ci-implement-role work because the issue remained triage:ready after the first spawn. The daemon's dedup TTL cache expired between spawns, allowing re-spawning of already-in-progress work. Root cause: daemon should mark issue in_progress immediately on spawn to prevent re-spawn. Redesign coaching metrics + integrate into orch stats + auto-surface at session start
description:
Three-part feature:

## 1. Metric Redesign

ORCHESTRATOR - Strip noise, keep signal:
- KEEP: frame_collapse (delegation rule violation), circular_pattern (contradicting prior knowledge)
- KEEP but tune: behavioral_variation (useful for spawn retry loops)
- KILL: action_ratio (378 fires, wrong signal for orchestrators), analysis_paralysis (352 fires, consecutive bash is normal), compensation_pattern (79 fires, too loose at 30% overlap), context_ratio (unclear value), dylan_signal_prefix (never fired), premise_skipping (never fired)
- ADD: spawn_without_context (no kb context in recent tool window before orch spawn), completion_backlog (agents at Phase:Complete >10 min without orch complete), direct_implementation (Edit/Write on files outside .kb/ .orch/ CLAUDE.md AGENTS.md)

Result: 5 orchestrator metrics (2 kept, 3 new) down from 8 noisy ones.

WORKER - Keep all 4 as-is, let them accumulate real data:
- tool_failure_rate (3+ consecutive failures)
- context_usage (estimated tokens, alert at 80%)
- time_in_phase (minutes since phase change, alert at 15+)
- commit_gap (time since last commit, alert at 30+ min)

## 2. Stats Integration

Add coaching summary section to orch stats output:
```
🧠 BEHAVIORAL HEALTH (coaching plugin, last N days)
  Orchestrator:
    Frame collapse:       3 events (2 sessions)
    Circular patterns:    1 event
    Completion backlog:   2 alerts
    Direct implementation: 0 events
  Workers:
    Context exhaustion:   2 alerts (>80%)
    Tool failure runs:    1 alert
    Stuck in phase:       0 alerts
    Commit gaps:          4 alerts
```

Read from ~/.orch/coaching-metrics.jsonl, aggregate by metric_type for the stats time window.

## 3. Session Start Auto-Surface

Run orch stats --days 1 summary automatically when orchestrator session starts. Could be:
- Plugin hook on first tool call in new session
- session.prompt.before hook if available
- Injection via coaching plugin detecting new orchestrator session

Goal: orchestrator sees system health picture without remembering to run the command.

## Evidence
- coaching-metrics.jsonl has 1000+ entries, dominated by action_ratio (378) and analysis_paralysis (352) noise
- Worker metrics just started working (Feb 14 fix) - context_usage verified
- orch stats already reads events.jsonl, adding coaching-metrics.jsonl pass is natural
- Current session start is skill doc guideline only, not automated

## Key files
- plugins/coaching.ts (metric detection, 1831 lines)
- cmd/orch/stats.go (stats aggregation)
- .opencode/plugin/coaching.ts (deployed copy, must stay in sync)

title: Improve daemon skill inference with description-aware heuristics and accuracy tracking
description: Two gaps in daemon skill inference (pkg/daemon/skill_inference.go): (1) DESCRIPTION-AWARE HEURISTICS: Current inference only uses issue type + skill:* labels + title prefix. title: Enrich spawn context with orch tree --cluster for area awareness
description:
Before spawning into an area, the orchestrator should see the full knowledge landscape for that area. This improves skill selection, prompt quality, and prevents duplicate work.

CONTEXT: Currently kb context provides keyword search. orch tree --cluster provides structural awareness — what investigations exist, what decisions were made, what is untested. Together they give the orchestrator full area context before spawning.

SCOPE:
1. When orchestrator runs orch spawn, auto-detect the area from issue labels or task description
2. If area maps to a known cluster, inject orch tree --cluster <area> --depth 3 output into the spawn prompt or show it to orchestrator before spawn confirmation
3. Add orch tree --cluster <area> --format summary flag that produces a 3-5 line area briefing:
   - N investigations, M decisions, K models, J open issues
   - Health smells if any
   - Most recent artifact date
4. Consider adding this to SPAWN_CONTEXT.md template so agents also see where their work fits

DEPENDS ON: health smells and relationship parsing for full value, but basic cluster summary works with current tree.

VERIFICATION:
- orch tree --cluster X --format summary produces concise area briefing
- Spawning into an area with a known cluster surfaces the cluster context
- Agents see where their work fits in the knowledge landscape
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

title: [orch-go] systematic-debugging: Build is broken: cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go:536:1 ha...
Reorganize orchestrator skill around 'keep Dylan oriented' as primary frame
description:
The orchestrator skill (~1,251 lines) is currently organized around what the orchestrator does (delegate, triage, synthesize). But Dylan's experience of working with an orchestrator should feel like being narrated through a journey, not presented with checkboxes. The completion review section describes a mechanical two-gate process, but Dylan reports the ceremony feels disconnected — he lacks the frame to understand why completed work matters by the time it arrives.

Core insight from conversation: the comprehension problem isn't at completion time, it's at spawn time. The 'why Dylan cares' frame decays between spawn and completion, and nothing preserves it. The explain-back gate assumes Dylan has context he's already lost.

Proposed reframe: organize the entire skill around 'is Dylan oriented?' at each moment:
- At spawn: establish the frame (what are we doing and why)
- During work: maintain narrative continuity (things are progressing)  
- At completion: reconnect to original motivation, resolve, place contextually (1 of 3 blockers for X)
- At session boundaries: 'previously on...' orientation

The mechanical constraints (delegation rule, verification gates, daemon management) don't disappear but become implementation detail in service of orientation, not the organizing frame.

Key constraint: the skill is auto-generated from templates at ~/orch-knowledge/skills/src/meta/orchestrator/.skillc/ — architect must propose structure changes to template sources, not SKILL.md directly.

Risk: current skill is battle-tested, every rule from a real failure. New frame must be at least as protective while being more coherent.

title: bd sync hangs — second occurrence in 24 hours
description: bd sync is hanging repeatedly. First occurrence: earlier today during agent completion flow. Second occurrence: just now, orch status also timed out (likely calls beads internally). Earlier we saw bd sync fail with 'cannot pull with rebase: You have unstaged changes' — but that should be an error, not a hang. Two possibilities: (1) git lock contention from multiple agents committing simultaneously, (2) actual deadlock in bd sync logic. The beads repo had a recent fix for bd sync deadlock (commit in beads repo). Need to verify we're running the patched version and investigate whether the hang is git-level or beads-level.

title: Model strategy: evaluate Claude model lineup for orchestration workflow
description: Sonnet 4.6 launched (Feb 17 2026) — approaches Opus-level intelligence at Sonnet pricing. Users preferred it to Opus 4.5 59% of the time. Less overengineering, better instruction following, 1M context window. Anthropic says 'Opus 4.6 remains the strongest for deepest reasoning.' We currently default to sonnet for headless spawns and use --opus for complex skills (debugging, investigation, architect, audit, research). Questions to answer: (1) Should Sonnet 4.6 replace Opus for any currently-Opus skills? (2) Is Opus 4.6 still worth the Max subscription cost for agent work? (3) What's the right model for each skill type given current pricing and capability? (4) Should we add Sonnet 4.6 as an explicit model option in orch spawn? (5) How does the 1M context window change our context management strategy? Reference: https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-sonnet-4-6 Current model selection: skill/meta/orchestrator SKILL.md Model Selection section.

title: Fix dashboard blind to tmux agents: reorder scans in serve_agents.go

title: Dashboard work-graph usability improvements: visibility, sorting, and clarity
description:
The work-graph Issues view is structurally working (dependency trees, Ready to Complete, independent issues) but has several usability gaps that make it hard to orient at a glance:

**1. In-progress issues don't visually stand out**
The play icon + green subline is too subtle. P1 in-progress bugs should pop — left border accent, background highlight, or similar treatment to the Ready to Complete section.

**2. P1s buried in INDEPENDENT ISSUES**
Two P1 bugs are mixed in with 25 P2s. Priority should sort within sections — P1s at top, not mixed by creation order.

**3. 'No active agent linked' is noisy and negative**
Shown on every in-progress issue (partly due to orch-go-1022 tmux bug). Even when fixed, the subline should show agent phase + runtime when linked, and just 'unassigned' or nothing when not — not an alarming message.

**4. Ready to Complete shows stale/already-closed entries**
980.1 (already closed) and 990 (failed Codex attempt with stale Phase: Complete) still appear. Section should only show issues that are actually open + Phase: Complete.

**5. 27 independent issues is overwhelming**
Most are backlog. Default should show active work (in-progress + recently created) and collapse/paginate the rest. Or separate 'Active' vs 'Backlog' sections.

**6. Completed agents section competes with Ready to Complete**
The completed agents banner (token counts, timing) is useful but visually competes with the more actionable Ready to Complete section. Consider collapsing by default or moving to Completed tab.

**7. Dependency chain header uses issue title as section name**
Chain header shows the title of issue 980.4 which reads oddly as a group name. Should use the root issue title, a computed chain name, or just the root issue ID.

title: Design and implement orch rework command for sending agents back with specific completion review feedback
description: Completion review regularly finds gaps in agent work (e.g. xu8l shipped without removing flag definitions, sg4k sort has no effect). title: 96 phantom agents and stale session metrics — root cause unknown
description: orch status shows 96 phantom agents and session metrics from a 689h-old session. Originally attributed to OpenCode API routing (orch-go-1033) but that was disproven — orch-go's client uses /session not /api/session and those endpoints work fine. Server restart reduced phantoms from showing but didn't eliminate them. Possible causes: 1) Sessions accumulate in OpenCode's session store and are never cleaned up. 2) orch's registry tracks sessions that no longer exist on the server. 3) The /session/status endpoint returns {} (empty) which may cause orch to classify all sessions as phantom. 4) 689h session metrics suggest the session tracking state file is never reset. Need to understand the full lifecycle: how does a session go from spawned → active → complete → cleaned up, and where is that chain breaking?

title: Investigation: skillc deploy pipeline is a source of chronic rework and confusion — audit source/compiled/deployed file relationships and fix
description: Repeated pattern: skill fixes get applied to the wrong file, deployed to the wrong location, or deployed correctly but not picked up by running sessions. This has caused at least 3 rounds of rework on the orchestrator skill alone (orch-go-1036, orch-go-1037, orch-go-1044). The problems: (1) Multiple copies of the same skill exist across different paths — source template, compiled intermediate, deployed copy, and sometimes stale copies in other directories. Nobody knows which file is authoritative. (2) Workers edit deployed artifacts instead of source templates because the relationship is unclear. skillc deploy then overwrites their work. (3) skillc deploy --target deploys to a root SKILL.md, not into the meta/orchestrator/ subdirectory where the old manual copy lives, creating two competing files. (4) Running sessions cache the skill at load time — deploying a fix has no effect until session restart, but there is no signal that this is the case. (5) orch-go-1015 (P3, open) already identified 5 stale orchestrator skill copies. The investigation should map the full lifecycle: source template → skillc compile → skillc deploy → session load → runtime. Identify every point of confusion and propose a simplified pipeline.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Fix headless spawn to verify session creation and surface prompt_async errors instead of silently reporting success.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Headless codex spawns report success but the agent is silently dead. orch-go fires CreateSession + prompt_async and walks away. If auth/model fails, the error is only logged server-side. User sees 'Spawned agent (headless)' when the agent is DOA.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Small

## Root Cause (from orch-go-1051)
1. prompt_async returns 204 immediately. Errors only emitted as session.error SSE events.
2. Session listing is project-scoped by x-opencode-directory header.
3. orch-go never verifies the session after creating it.

## The Fix
In pkg/opencode/client.go: After CreateSession + SendMessageInDirectory, verify session exists with GET /session/:id and correct directory header. Brief SSE subscribe (2-3s) to catch session.error. Return error if session dead.

In pkg/orch/extraction.go (headless spawn path): Check verification result. Print error instead of success if session is dead.

## Scope
IN: Post-spawn verification in orch-go. Surface errors.
OUT: No OpenCode server changes.

## Verification
go test ./pkg/opencode/ ./pkg/orch/

title: [orch-go] systematic-debugging: Fix GPT/codex agents not committing work or rep...

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Fix GPT/codex agents not committing work or reporting phase.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Dylan spent an entire day where 16 of 20 price-watch agents did their work but never committed or reported phase. The code was on disk but never git committed. We just spawned an opus agent to fix this and IT DID THE SAME THING — wrote SYNTHESIS claiming success but made zero file changes. The problem is self-demonstrating.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Small

## Root Cause (already identified by prior agent)

The SPAWN_CONTEXT.md template in pkg/spawn/context.go says 'After your final commit' but NEVER includes explicit git add and git commit commands. Anthropic models infer this. GPT models do not.

## The Fix (simple and specific)

In pkg/spawn/context.go, find the SESSION COMPLETE PROTOCOL sections and add explicit git commit instructions with code blocks. There are 3 sections that need updating:

1. Main tracked spawn completion (~lines 162-204) — for both light and full tier
2. NoTrack/ad-hoc spawn completion (~lines 136-160)  
3. Final step reminder at end of template (~lines 399-428)

Add to each section, BEFORE the bd comment and /exit steps:
```
git add -A && git commit -m 'feat/fix: <description of changes>'
```

Also update pkg/spawn/context_test.go to expect the new commit instructions.

## Scope
IN: Edit pkg/spawn/context.go and pkg/spawn/context_test.go. Run tests. COMMIT YOUR CHANGES.
OUT: Nothing else.

## CRITICAL: YOU MUST COMMIT
After making changes:
1. git add -A
2. git commit -m 'fix: add explicit git commit instructions to spawn context for GPT model compliance'
3. go test ./pkg/spawn/ ./pkg/orch/
4. bd comment add orch-go-49025 'Phase: Complete - ...' (this may fail if issue doesn't exist, that's OK)

## Verification
go test ./pkg/spawn/ ./pkg/orch/

title: Rethink agent observability architecture — current multi-source discovery is unfixable

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Strategic redesign: agent observability architecture.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Dylan spent all of Feb 18 firefighting observability bugs — price-watch agents reported as dead when they were running fine, 84/85 agents showing empty metadata in dashboard, cross-repo visibility broken despite 6 weeks of fixes. The incremental fix approach has failed. We need a clean design.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Large

## Context

The current agent observability layer has 5 interacting data sources:
1. Workspace scanning (.orch/workspace/ directories with SPAWN_CONTEXT.md)
2. Tmux window discovery (scanning for emoji-prefixed window names)  
3. OpenCode session API (HTTP queries to localhost:4096)
4. Beads enrichment (reading phase comments from beads issues)
5. Agent registry / state.db (legacy, stale, not written to)

These are reconciled in serve_agents.go and serve_agents_cache.go. The reconciliation is where every bug lives.

## Key Constraints Discovered

- OpenCode --attach mode sets session directory from server's cwd, not CLI's cwd (cross-project agents all show orch-go as directory)
- kb projects integration adds price-watch to workspace scanning, but enrichment fails silently
- Workspace scan and tmux scan can claim the same agent with different metadata
- Beads enrichment is skipped for workspace-scanned entries (performance optimization that breaks correctness)
- Agent registry hasn't been written to today; state.db is 6 days stale — both are effectively dead

## Strategic Questions to Answer

1. Should there be ONE authoritative source of truth for agent state? If so, which one?
2. What's the minimal data model the dashboard needs? (id, status, phase, project, model — what else?)
3. Can we eliminate the multi-source reconciliation entirely?
4. How should cross-repo agents work? (spawned from orch-go, running in price-watch)
5. What happens when the source of truth is unavailable? (graceful degradation vs hard failure)

## Artifacts to Read

- cmd/orch/serve_agents.go — current multi-source agent discovery
- cmd/orch/serve_agents_cache.go — workspace cache with kb projects integration
- cmd/orch/serve_beads.go — beads graph API with active_agent
- pkg/opencode/client.go — OpenCode session queries
- .orch/workspace/archived/og-inv-dashboard-blind-claude-17feb-24d6-213340/SYNTHESIS.md — root cause of scan ordering bug
- .orch/workspace/archived/og-debug-fix-dashboard-blind-17feb-1b59/SYNTHESIS.md — fix that didn't stick
- .orch/workspace/archived/og-arch-cross-project-agents-07jan-1844/SYNTHESIS.md — cross-project design
- .orch/workspace/archived/og-debug-investigate-headless-codex-18feb-0eb3/SYNTHESIS.md — headless visibility

## Deliverable

An investigation with a clear recommendation for a simplified agent observability architecture. Should include:
- Proposed single source of truth (or argued case for why multi-source is necessary)
- Data model for agent state
- How cross-repo agents are tracked
- Migration path from current to proposed
- What gets deleted vs kept

Write to: .kb/investigations/2026-02-18-design-agent-observability-rethink.md

title: Implement work-centric agent discovery — invert from infrastructure-first to beads-first
description:
Core observability rewrite. Current serve_agents.go discovers agents from infrastructure (OpenCode sessions, tmux windows) then reconciles 5 data sources to figure out what work they represent. This is backwards and the reconciliation is where every bug lives (~1400 lines).

New model: Start from beads issues (status=in_progress), find workspace by beads_id, get session_id from workspace, check OpenCode for liveness only. O(n) single-pass instead of O(n*m) reconciliation.

Combined approach from two architects:
- Architect A (Feb 14): Move state into OpenCode sessions — infrastructure half-built (session metadata API, AGENT_MANIFEST.json)
- Architect B (Feb 18): Invert discovery — start from beads (work), not sessions (infrastructure)

Key artifacts:
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-18-design-agent-observability-rethink.md
- .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-lifecycle-ownership-own-accept-build.md
- .orch/workspace/og-arch-strategic-redesign-agent-18feb-0d2d/SYNTHESIS.md

Depends on: orch-go-1059 (delete dead registry first), orch-go-1060 (cross-project fix informs rewrite)

title: Wire OpenCode session metadata into agent discovery — complete the half-built bridge from Feb 14 decision
description:
The Feb 14 decision (.kb/decisions/2026-02-14-lifecycle-ownership-own-accept-build.md) designed a 3-step plan to move agent state into OpenCode sessions. The OpenCode fork already has the features built:
- Session metadata API (commit 36f084ca5) — store beads_id, workspace, tier
- Session TTL (commit f3c3865b8) — configurable expiry
- Worker detection (commits 459a1bfba, 0922edfe7)
- State.create cache fix (commit b6823f82b)

And orch-go spawn already calls CreateSession() with metadata and writes AGENT_MANIFEST.json.

But serve_agents.go never consumed any of this. After 1061 lands (beads-first discovery), the next step is wiring the OpenCode session metadata as the liveness/identity lookup instead of the current SSE polling and session list scanning.

Steps from the decision:
1. Wire GET /session/status endpoint (eliminates ~1,400 lines of SSE status parsing)
2. Read session metadata for beads_id/workspace/tier (eliminates ~800 lines of workspace cross-reference)
3. Use session TTL for cleanup (eliminates ~1,200 lines of cleanup code)

Key artifacts:
- .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-lifecycle-ownership-own-accept-build.md
- .kb/models/opencode-fork.md
- pkg/opencode/client.go — already has CreateSession with metadata

title: Add integration tests for beads-first agent discovery in serve_agents.go
description:
orch-go-1061 rewrote agent discovery to start from beads issues instead of scanning OpenCode sessions/tmux. Zero tests were written. This is the same pattern that caused the Feb 18 nightmare — code ships without tests, breaks weeks later, fix causes another break.

Test coverage needed for the new discovery path:

1. discoverAgentsFromWork / listInProgressIssues:
   - Single-project: returns in_progress issues, ignores open/closed
   - Cross-project: queries multiple project dirs, deduplicates
   - Empty project dirs: falls back to default ListOpenIssues
   - Error handling: one project fails, others still returned

2. handleAgents beads-first loop:
   - Workspace found: session_id, spawn_time, project_dir populated from workspace
   - Workspace not found: agent still returned with beads data only
   - Session found: status derived from activity (active/idle/dead thresholds)
   - Session not found: agent shows as dead
   - Cross-project: correct project_dir from workspace, not session.directory

3. Cache behavior:
   - projectDirsMatchForIDs: cache invalidates when project context changes
   - Beads cache keyed by project dir (from orch-go-1060)

4. Edge cases:
   - --no-track agents (no beads issue)
   - Completed agents with SYNTHESIS.md
   - Stale agent filtering (displayThreshold)
   - Agent with beads issue but no workspace yet (just spawned)

Key files:
- cmd/orch/serve_agents.go — the code to test
- cmd/orch/serve_agents_test.go — existing tests to extend
- cmd/orch/serve_agents_cache.go — cache logic
- cmd/orch/serve_agents_cache_test.go — existing cache tests

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Every few days a new bug surfaces where user intent (model, backend, mode) gets lost or overridden somewhere in the spawn pipeline. We've fixed 10 of these individually. The root cause is there's no single config resolution function — user config, project config, explicit flags, infrastructure escape hatch, and daemon defaults all compete with no defined precedence.

ARCHITECT SCOPE:
- Audit all config sources that affect spawn: explicit CLI flags, user config (~/.orch/config.yaml), project config (.orch/config.yaml), infrastructure escape hatch, daemon defaults
- Design a single precedence model (likely: explicit flags > user config > project config > defaults)
- Design a single resolution function that every spawn path calls
- Account for the infrastructure escape hatch (when/whether it should override)
- Identify which of the 3 open bugs (1047, 1048, 1049) this design would close

PRIOR ART (closed bugs in this area):
- orch-go-1011: default_model forced wrong backend
- orch-go-1020: explicit flags overridden by escape hatch
- orch-go-1028: daemon ignores default_model
- orch-go-1045: escape hatch overrides user config
- orch-go-1046: full audit investigation (read this first)
- orch-go-1051, 1055: headless spawn silent failures

OPEN BUGS this should address:
- orch-go-1047: project spawn_mode overrides user backend
- orch-go-1048: malformed userconfig silently ignored
- orch-go-1049: user default_model not treated as explicit

DELIVERABLE: Architecture decision in .kb/decisions/ with precedence model, resolution function signature, and migration path from current ad-hoc resolution.

title: Audit entropy spiral for recoverable features by functional area
description:
## Context
Reverted orch-go to Jan 18 baseline (0bca3dec) after entropy spiral (1163 agent commits, 5.4M LOC churn). Evidence preserved on entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch. Some features built during the spiral were genuinely valuable and should be recovered.

## Approach
Diff Jan 18 (0bca3dec) vs entropy-spiral-feb2026 by functional area. For each area, identify:
- Net-new features (not just churn/refactor cycles)
- Whether the feature compiled/worked at HEAD of spiral branch
- Dependencies on other spiral-era code
- Recovery complexity (clean extract vs needs adaptation)

## Areas to audit
1. **Web UI** - /work-graph route, dashboard agent cards, vite config changes
2. **Spawn system** - Backend abstraction (pkg/spawn/backends/), new flags, spawn pipeline
3. **Completion pipeline** - Pipeline decomposition, skip config, retry logic, SkipConfig type
4. **Daemon** - ProcessedIssueCache, skill inference improvements, processed-cache
5. **Observability** - SSE improvements, attention system (pkg/attention/), churn metrics
6. **Knowledge system** - Models/probes Go code (already recovering separately), kb reflect
7. **CLI commands** - Any new subcommands that were useful (orch phase, orch lint, etc.)

## Deliverables
- Recovery priority list: which features to recover first based on value vs complexity
- For each recommended recovery: exact commits/files to cherry-pick
- Flag features that depend on each other (can't recover X without Y)

## Method
Use git diff, git log, and file analysis on the entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch.
Do NOT modify any code - this is a read-only audit.

title: Recover probe/model/investigation lifecycle decision from entropy spiral branch
description: Dylan's preferred design: 3+ investigations on the same domain warrant creating a model, and from then on probes are made against the model (not standalone investigations). This was a design or decision artifact on the entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch that was lost in the rollback. Agent should search entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch commits for this artifact and recover it. The current decision (2026-02-14-probe-as-universal-evidence-gathering-primitive.md) proposes retiring investigations entirely — Dylan's actual design preserves investigations as the entry point that graduate into models, with probes as the confirmatory mechanism against established models. Key distinction: investigations are for novel exploration, probes are for testing model claims — NOT collapsing both into probes.

title: orch clean --sessions deletes active TUI/interactive sessions idle >5min
description:
Problem: cleanUntrackedDiskSessions() in clean_cmd.go:408-539 deletes sessions not tracked by any .orch/workspace/*/session_id file. TUI/interactive sessions have no workspace, so they bypass Layer 1 protection entirely. If user hasnt sent a message in >5min, Layer 2 (recency) marks session as orphaned and deletes it.

Code path: orch clean --sessions -> cleanUntrackedDiskSessions() -> !trackedSessionIDs[session.ID] (TUI has no workspace, always true) -> now.Sub(updatedAt) > 5min (user paused, true) -> skips IsSessionProcessing (only checked for recent sessions) -> client.DeleteSession (SESSION DELETED).

Fix: Call IsSessionProcessing() for ALL untracked sessions before deletion, not just recently active ones. Cost: one API call per untracked session. Minimal fix using existing Layer 3 defense thats currently short-circuited.

Evidence: Model .kb/models/session-deletion-vectors.md (Vector #2). Investigation .kb/investigations/2026-02-13-inv-verify-whether-orch-clean-kills-headless-sessions.md. Primary source: cmd/orch/clean_cmd.go:464-485.

title: Architect: Design accretion gravity enforcement infrastructure
description:
## Problem

Accretion Gravity is a proven principle (spawn_cmd.go grew from reasonable to 2,332 lines as 25+ agents each added features where similar code existed), but it has ZERO enforcement infrastructure. It's a reminder, not a gate — violating Gate Over Remind.

The principle identifies structural constraints as the fix:
- Explicit boundaries in CLAUDE.md ("new logic goes in pkg/, not cmd/")
- File-level rules ("if adding >10 lines to X, create a new package")
- Architectural review at completion, not just functional review

But none of these exist as infrastructure yet. Agents still freely accrete.

## What Needs Designing

Design the enforcement mechanisms that make Accretion Gravity a gate, not a reminder. Consider:

1. **Detection**: How do we detect accretion in real-time (not post-mortem)?
   - File size thresholds in completion verification?
   - Line-count delta checks in pre-commit hooks?
   - Hotspot analysis already exists but only warns

2. **Prevention**: How do we prevent accretion before it happens?
   - CLAUDE.md boundaries that agents actually respect?
   - Spawn context that includes "DO NOT add to these files" directives?
   - File-level extraction rules?

3. **Enforcement**: Where do gates live?
   - Completion verification (orch complete)?
   - Pre-commit hooks?
   - Coaching plugin real-time detection?
   - Spawn-time directives?

4. **Integration**: How does this compose with existing infrastructure?
   - Hotspot analysis (already detects bloated files)
   - Coaching plugin (already injects real-time feedback)
   - Completion verification (already gates on deliverables)
   - Code extraction patterns guide (already documents how to extract)

## Deliverable

Investigation with concrete design recommendations. NOT implementation — just the architecture of enforcement.

## Evidence

- spawn_cmd.go: 2,332 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- session.go: 2,166 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- doctor.go: 1,912 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- coaching.ts: 1,830 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- Principle provenance: .kb/decisions/2026-02-12-strangler-fig-refactor-cmd-orch.md
- Existing guide: .kb/guides/code-extraction-patterns.md

description:
**Problem:** `orch daemon preview` reports 'Verification backlog: 7 unverified completions from previous sessions' and pauses the daemon. But `orch review` shows only 1 pending completion. The remaining 6 are invisible to the review workflow, meaning the operator has no way to clear the backlog through the intended path.

**Impact:** Daemon is paused and can't spawn `triage:ready` work (specifically orch-go-v2dv, the Phase 2 heartbeat issue). The only escape is `orch daemon resume` which bypasses verification entirely — defeating the purpose of the constraint.

**Likely cause:** `SeedFromBacklog()` in `pkg/daemon/verification_tracker.go:109` counts agents that `orch review` filters out (already-closed issues, phantom agents, or agents completed without beads tracking). The two systems use different definitions of 'unverified completion'.

**Expected:** Tracker backlog count should match what `orch review` surfaces — the operator should be able to clear the backlog by completing everything in the review queue.

**Reference:** This is a verifiability-first enforcement gap — the gate fires correctly but the release mechanism is broken.

title: Rework daemon: no auto-close, faster polling, review-queue model
description:
Two changes to daemon behavior based on verifiability-first design session:

1. DAEMON NEVER AUTO-CLOSES ISSUES
Current: daemon polls for Phase:Complete, verifies artifacts, closes issue automatically.
New: daemon detects Phase:Complete, flags agent as 'ready for review', but does NOT close the issue. Dylan closes issues through the orchestrator after two-gate verification (comprehension + behavioral). Daemon pauses spawning when N agents are waiting for review (default 3) so work doesnt pile up unboundedly.

This means the daemon loop becomes:
  - Poll bd ready, spawn within capacity
  - Monitor for Phase:Complete
  - When agents complete, mark as ready-for-review (not closed)
  - Pause spawning when N agents waiting for review
  - Dylan reviews batch through orchestrator, closes each
  - Daemon sees capacity freed, spawns more

2. FASTER POLL INTERVAL
Current: 60s poll interval feels sluggish. Dashboard doesnt update quickly after CLI commands.
New: Drop poll interval to 15-20s. The daemon is just doing lightweight beads queries - this is not a meaningful load increase. Also reduce spawn delay from 10s to 3-5s (dedup cache already prevents duplicates).

Why both together: the faster polling makes the system feel responsive when Dylan is actively working. The no-auto-close ensures faster polling doesnt mean faster unverified closures.

REMOVES: daemon auto-completion loop (or repurpose it to only flag ready-for-review, not close)
KEEPS: VerificationTracker pause logic (but now its 'pause after N ready-for-review' not 'pause after N auto-completions')
KEEPS: orch daemon resume command

Prior work: 85a0e021 (verification tracker), design session Feb 14 (verifiability-first constraint)
Supersedes the auto-completion behavior from the current daemon.go completion loop.

title: Update skill system for verifiability-first: explain-back scaffolding and human_verified gate
description:
Update the orchestrator skill (skills/src/meta/orchestrator/) to implement completion review as the primary orchestrator workflow.

## What Completion Review Is

Completion review is the two-gate process Dylan uses to engage with agent output. Every completed agent goes through this before closing. This is the orchestrator's primary job — not dispatching work, but understanding what was done.

### Gate 1: Comprehension (Explain-Back)

The orchestrator presents 'the what' — factual scaffolding about what was built/found/decided, in plain language. Dylan explains 'the so-what' — why it matters, what it changes, what concerns him.

Design principles:
- Safe to be wrong (Dylan should feel comfortable saying 'I don't get it')
- Not starting from nothing (orchestrator provides the what)
- Not given the answer (orchestrator does NOT present the so-what)
- No sycophancy: do NOT mirror Dylan's words back, do NOT validate incomplete understanding, do NOT say 'great explanation' when the answer is partial
- If Dylan's explanation misses something important, say so directly
- If Dylan asks a question that reveals genuine understanding, that counts

### Gate 2: Behavioral (Tiered by Work Type)

After comprehension passes:

| Type | Behavioral Gate |
|------|----------------|
| Features | Run it, see it work |
| Bug fixes | Reproduce bug, confirm fixed |  
| Investigations | Spot-check one claim against reality |
| Decisions | N/A (comprehension IS the gate) |

### Pacing

Not every completion review is heavy. Friction scales with complexity/risk:
- Light completions (typo, config, formatting): fast explain-back, minimal behavioral
- Medium completions (single feature, known pattern): standard explain-back, run it
- Heavy completions (architectural change, new subsystem, design decision): thorough explain-back, careful behavioral verification

### Anti-Patterns to Prevent

- Rubber-stamping: orchestrator accepting 'yeah that makes sense' without probing
- Sycophancy: 'Great insight!' when Dylan gives a surface-level answer  
- Ceremony theater: going through the motions on trivial completions
- Skipping comprehension on investigations/decisions because 'artifact is deliverable'
- Presenting options then asking 'which would you pick?' (that's giving the answer)

### Integration Points

- The orchestrator skill's SYNTHESIZE section needs to be rewritten around completion review
- The 'Investigation Outcomes' section needs to include spot-check behavioral gate
- The autonomy section needs to clarify: orchestrator acts autonomously on dispatching, but NEVER skips completion review
- orch complete CLI: --explain flag accepts the explain-back summary (conversational, not CLI prompts)

### What to Modify

Primary file: the orchestrator skill source in skills/src/meta/orchestrator/
Specifically the SYNTHESIZE section, completion review protocol, and investigation outcomes.

Also update worker-base shared skill to require plain-language summary in SYNTHESIS.md (this becomes the scaffolding for explain-back).

### References

- kb-158420 (human verification required)
- kb-320fb2 (explain-back gate) 
- kb-afa611 (sycophancy constraint)
- kb-d4e04d (explain-back is conversational not CLI)
- kb-286915 (completion review model: all types get comprehension gate)
- kb-853ff6 (fail-fast constraint on daemon spawn prerequisites)
- .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md

title: Redesign control plane circuit breaker heuristics - daily commit count is wrong signal
description: The circuit breaker fired on its first day (59/20 commits) during a normal batch review session. The threshold of 20 commits/day is too aggressive for normal operation (3-4 agents produce this easily). The entropy spiral was 45 commits/day sustained for 26 days — the signal isn't 'too many commits today' but 'too many commits sustained over multiple days without human verification.' The heuristic needs to detect sustained unverified velocity, not single-day spikes. Consider: rolling window (3-day average), human-verified vs agent-only commit distinction, commits-without-human-interaction metric, or graduated warnings before halt. Also: the post-commit hook counts ALL commits including orchestrator cherry-picks from agent workspaces, inflating the count.

Daemon behavior change: pause after N completions without human verification
description: Current daemon behavior: polls bd ready, auto-spawns indefinitely. This produced the 26-day spiral (1163 commits, 24/7 autonomous operation, zero human involvement). New behavior: daemon tracks completions since last human verification. After N completions without human activity, daemon PAUSES and waits. Not alerts - actually stops spawning. Dylan explicitly resumes when he is back and has reviewed completed work. This is the mechanical enforcement of the verifiability-first constraint. Design considerations: (1) What counts as human activity? Explain-back in orch complete conversation, not just a CLI flag. (2) What is N? Start conservative (e.g., 3 completions). (3) Daemon should surface what is waiting for review when Dylan returns. Prior art: every post-mortem recommended this, none implemented it.
notes:
DESIGN SESSION CONTEXT (Feb 14):

Dylan's insight: daemon pause isn't just safety - it's a workflow design tool. Batching completions into review sessions means: sit down, review N things with two-gate verification (comprehension + behavioral), release next batch, walk away. Sustainable rhythm, not constant availability.

Connects to two-gate verification (orch-go-tyi): daemon pauses after N completions. When Dylan returns, he reviews each completion through comprehension gate (what/so-what) then behavioral gate (tiered). Only after verification does system proceed.

Dylan accepts the slowdown if it means quality and understanding.

title: Investigate: which skills produce investigation artifacts and should they?
description:
## Problem

936 investigations exist, only 3 synthesized (312:1 ratio). 45 created in February alone. Investigation production far outpaces synthesis capacity.

Hypothesis: too many skills are configured to produce .kb/investigations/ artifacts when they shouldn't need to. Investigation files should be reserved for genuine exploratory/understanding work, not as a default output for every skill.

## What to check

1. Audit all skill definitions in ~/.claude/skills/ — which ones list investigation artifacts as outputs/deliverables?
2. For each skill that produces investigations: is that appropriate, or should the skill produce a different artifact (or none)?
3. Check skill.yaml or SKILL.md `outputs.required` fields across all skills
4. Check spawn context generation — does it default to investigation deliverables?

## Also check

- Are agents creating investigations opportunistically even when not asked to? (e.g., feature-impl agents creating investigations as side effects)
- Is the investigation template/path being injected into spawn context unnecessarily?

## Deliverable

List of skills that produce investigations, recommendation for which should stop, and what they should produce instead (if anything).

## Evidence

- 936 total investigations, 3 synthesized, 72 archived
- 45 investigations in February 2026 alone
- kb reflect shows 29 synthesis opportunities across 12+ clusters

title: Design: rethink orch spawn flag semantics (model/backend/mode conflation)
description:
## Revised Design: orch spawn flag simplification

### Core Insight
Model and backend are fully independent axes. The opus→claude coupling was a false constraint — opus works through OpenCode API. The --opus flag has no reason to exist.

### Final Design (from completion review)

**Two independent axes:**
1. **Model** (`--model`): what LLM to use. Aliases resolved via config (`~/.orch/config.yaml`).
2. **Backend** (`--backend`): how to run the session (opencode API / claude CLI).

**Visibility:** `--tmux` is the only opt-in. Default is headless. `--inline` and `--attach` removed.

**Config-based model aliases:**
```yaml
models:
  opus: anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
  sonnet: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929
  flash: google/gemini-3-flash-preview
```

**Removed:**
- `--opus` flag (just use `--model opus`)
- `--inline` flag (never used)
- `--attach` flag (just use tmux directly)
- model→backend auto-selection (false constraint)

**Fixed:**
- Infrastructure detection: advisory only, explicit `--backend` always wins
- `--mode` validation: reject 'claude'/'opencode' values with helpful error
- Default backend: opencode (matches daemon common case)

### Implementation issues
- orch-go-XXXX: Phase 1 (non-breaking fixes)
- orch-go-XXXX: Phase 2 (flag removal + config aliases)

### Reference
- Design synthesis: .orch/workspace/og-work-rethink-orch-spawn-15feb-d93f/SYNTHESIS.md
- False constraint: kb-0b3f89 (opus does NOT require claude backend)

title: Probe: do agents naturally route to extracted packages without being told?
description:
## Hypothesis

After extracting spawn gates to pkg/spawn/gates/, agents given spawn-gate-related tasks will naturally find and add code to the extracted packages — NOT to spawn_cmd.go — without being told where to put it.

## Protocol

1. Wait for extraction (orch-go extract spawn gates issue) to complete
2. Spawn a feature-impl agent with a task like "add rate-limit gate to spawn" or "add file-size pre-check to spawn"
3. CRITICAL: Do NOT mention pkg/spawn/gates/ in the spawn context. Let the agent discover the codebase.
4. After completion, check: where did the agent add code?

## Success criteria

- Agent found pkg/spawn/gates/ and added code there → CONFIRMS hypothesis
- Agent added to spawn_cmd.go anyway → CONTRADICTS hypothesis, need stronger signals

## What to record

- Which files the agent created/modified (git diff)
- Whether the agent mentioned discovering pkg/spawn/gates/ in its reasoning
- Total lines added to spawn_cmd.go vs pkg/spawn/gates/

## Constraint

This is an ORCHESTRATOR probe — the orchestrator runs this manually after extraction completes. Do NOT spawn this as autonomous work. The value is in observing agent behavior, not automating the observation.

title: Architect: Knowledge lineage tree visualization for dashboard and orchestrator
description:
Design a tree-based knowledge lineage visualization that shows the natural parent-child relationships between knowledge artifacts (investigations, decisions, models, probes, guides) and the beads issues they spawn.

The tree structure falls out naturally from existing data:
- Investigations reference prior work (Prior-Work table)
- Decisions cite investigations (Evidence section)
- Issues have descriptions pointing to decisions
- Probes live inside model directories (filesystem hierarchy)
- Models synthesize from investigations (Synthesized From)

Example rendering (Unicode box-drawing tree):

orch-go knowledge tree
│
├─◉ Entropy Spiral
│ ├─◉ post-mortems/2026-01-02-system-spiral-dec27-jan02.md
│ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-12-inv-entropy-spiral-postmortem.md
│ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-14-inv-entropy-spiral-deep-analysis.md
│ │ ├─★ decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md
│ │ │ ├─● orch-go-5sc  Decision record                    CLOSED
│ │ │ ├─● orch-go-7jl  Daemon pause after N completions   CLOSED
│ │ │ └─◇ orch-go-6th  Skill system update                triage:review
│ │ └─● orch-go-agr  Trajectory audit                     IN PROGRESS
│ └─◉ handoffs/2026-02-13-entropy-spiral-recovery.md

DESIGN QUESTIONS:
1. Where does this render? Dashboard (Svelte, persistent, browsable), orchestrator session (inline, contextual during verification), or both?
2. Data model: How to extract the tree from existing artifacts? Parse Prior-Work tables, Synthesized From headers, beads deps, filesystem hierarchy? Or add explicit lineage metadata?
3. Interaction: Click to expand/collapse? Filter by area? Highlight verification state (verified vs pending)? Show health smells (15 investigations with no decision = cluster smell)?
4. Integration with verify flow: When daemon pauses and Dylan reviews, show the tree with new nodes highlighted so he sees where work fits before explain-back begins.
5. CLI vs web: Should 'orch tree' or 'kb tree' exist as CLI command (for orchestrator session rendering) in addition to dashboard?
6. Grouping: The top-level clusters (Entropy Spiral, Coaching Plugin, etc.) - are these inferred from area: labels, filesystem paths, or explicit topic tags?

USE CASES:
- Session start: see the knowledge landscape, find where to focus
- Triage: spot clusters with many investigations but no decisions (smell)
- Verification: see where a completed deliverable fits before explain-back
- Orientation: new session Claude sees the tree, immediately understands project structure

CONSTRAINTS:
- Must work with existing .kb/ file structure and beads issue data
- Tree relationships already implicit in artifacts - design should extract, not require manual curation
- Dylan doesnt read Go - the tree IS his primary interface for understanding project state

Prior art: The tree rendering above was produced in conversation and Dylan said 'wow, this is a really cool view' - that specific format resonated.

title: Daemon verification tracker reads labels instead of checkpoint file — enforcement gap
description:
PROBLEM: orch daemon run --dry-run reports 'Verification check: 0/3 unverified completions' despite verification-checkpoints.jsonl containing 13 entries with gate2_complete:false. The verification pause gate — the flagship control-plane mechanism from the verifiability-first decision — is not firing.

ROOT CAUSE: SeedFromBacklog() counts issues with daemon:ready-review label (via issue_adapter.go:168). When issues are closed via orch complete, the label is removed. So closed-but-unverified completions disappear from the daemon's view. The checkpoint file persists the unverified state correctly, but nothing reads it for enforcement.

This is a composition bug: checkpoint writing works (gate1/gate2 recorded correctly), label-based counting works (counts labels accurately), but they measure different things. Labels track active review queue. Checkpoints track verification state. The daemon uses the wrong one for enforcement.

FIX: The daemon's verification check should read verification-checkpoints.jsonl directly and count entries where gate2_complete is false (for Tier 1 work) or gate1_complete is false (for Tier 2 work). SeedFromBacklog should be replaced or updated to use the checkpoint file as source of truth, not labels.

VERIFICATION: After fix, orch daemon run --dry-run should report 13/3 unverified completions (or whatever the real count is) and refuse to spawn. The checkpoint file IS the source of truth — labels are the view layer.

EVIDENCE: Observed during bootstrap verification sequence (orch-go-hi8g). This confirms the bootstrap model prediction that gates may exist but not fire.

Reference: .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md (Constraint 3: Mechanical Enforcement)

description: The daemon extraction logic (pkg/daemon/extraction.go) has no memory of prior extractions. CheckExtractionNeeded() runs on every issue every poll cycle. title: Extraction issues recursively trigger more extraction via parseable file paths in titles

Fix: either exclude extraction issues from extraction checks (skip titles starting with 'Extract'), or remove file paths from generated titles.

title: Phase 2: Daemon model-drift reflection with throttled issue creation
description: Phase 2 of knowledge maintenance automation loop (.kb/decisions/2026-02-15-knowledge-maintenance-automation-loop.md). Daemon reads staleness events from ~/.orch/model-staleness-events.jsonl and creates beads issues when spawn-count >= 3 for same model. Add ReflectModelDriftEnabled and ReflectModelDriftInterval (default 4h) to daemon Config. Add RunModelDriftReflection() that: 1) reads staleness events, 2) counts per model, 3) deduplicates against existing open issues, 4) respects backpressure (max 3 open model-maintenance issues), 5) batches related models in same domain. Label created issues: model-maintenance. Priority: P2 for deleted files or >=5 commits changed, P3 for minor changes. Circuit breaker: halt creation if 5+ open model-maintenance issues. Acceptance: daemon creates model-update issue after 3+ stale spawns for same model; stops creating when 3 open.

Fix the inference to respect title prefixes (Architect:, Debug:, etc.) as strong skill signals. Small, surgical fix.

Daemon: auto-spawn extraction before feature work on CRITICAL hotspots
description:
## Goal

When daemon processes a triage:ready issue that targets a CRITICAL hotspot file (>1,500 lines), automatically spawn an extraction agent first to create the attractor package, then spawn the feature agent.

## How it works

1. Daemon picks up issue from triage:ready queue
2. Daemon runs hotspot check (already exists) against issue description/title to infer target files
3. If target file is CRITICAL (>1,500 lines): spawn extraction agent first, block feature spawn until extraction completes
4. If target file is fine: spawn feature agent normally

## Extraction agent task

"Extract [inferred concern] from [file] into [pkg/appropriate/package/]. Pure structural extraction — no behavior changes."

The extraction creates the attractor. Then the feature agent discovers it naturally (proven by probe orch-go-8ao: agent found pkg/spawn/gates/ without being told).

## Evidence

- Probe confirmed: agents naturally route to extracted packages (orch-go-8ao)
- spawn_cmd.go shrank from 2,332 → 1,973 lines across two extractions
- Agent even pulled existing inline code into the attractor package

## Constraints

- Don't over-engineer the file targeting — title/description keyword matching is fine for v1
- Extraction agent should be small-scoped (one concern at a time)
- If hotspot detection can't determine target file, skip and spawn normally

Prevent duplicate issue creation: title-based dedup on bd create
description:
**Problem:** beads allows creating multiple issues with identical titles. The daemon's extraction path shells out to `bd create` and can create N issues with the same title but different IDs. This is the root cause of 5x duplicate spawns (orch-go-e0o3).

**Current state:** `CreateArgs.Force` exists in the Go client but isn't used by orch-go's extraction path (which uses CLI). No title-based dedup exists at the beads layer.

**Desired behavior:** `bd create` should check for existing open issues with the same title before creating a new one. If a match exists, return the existing issue ID instead of creating a duplicate. A `--force` flag allows explicit override when duplicates are intentional.

**Defense in depth:** orch-go already has a downstream safety net (title-based dedup at spawn time, orch-go-e0o3). This beads-side fix prevents the duplicates from being created in the first place.

**Evidence:** 5 identical issues created: orch-go-l8k2, kzqq, pg9l, cu0r, xy7n — all 'Extract spawn flags phase 1: --mode'. Each spawned a separate agent that found work already done.

description: Phase 4 of verifiability-first decision. When verification constraints are violated, the system needs structured response: 1) Violation log (~/.orch/metrics/verification-violations.log) — records every violation with timestamp, beads_id, constraint violated, reason. 2) orch control status command — shows current verification state: unverified completions, heartbeat age, daemon paused/running, override count. 3) orch control resume --verified 'explanation' — explicit override to resume after violation halt. Architect: Probe as primary evidence artifact — retire investigations
description: Dylan values the probe concept because it forces specificity: 'test this claim' vs 'look into this area'. Investigations feel like one-offs disconnected from models, while probes create granularity useful for thinking ('what probes do we need before deciding?'). But the current system creates friction: Dylan has to decide 'investigation or probe?' before requesting evidence-gathering, and the routing logic is split across orchestrator skill (spawn-time detection) and investigation skill (mode detection from SPAWN_CONTEXT markers). Design question: should probes become the universal evidence-gathering primitive, with investigations retired? Key constraints: (1) probes currently must target a model — would need to support targeting any claim source (model, decision, assumption, code), (2) probes currently live nested under models (.kb/models/{name}/probes/) — may need their own location for discoverability, (3) investigation skill has significant infrastructure (D.E.K.N., Prior Work tables, self-review) that probes lack, (4) ~800+ existing investigations in .kb/investigations/ need migration/coexistence strategy. Dylan's framing: 'I should be thinking about what I want to understand, not which artifact shape to use.' The routing decision should be invisible to the meta-orchestrator.

Daemon dedup fundamentally broken: same spawn flags extraction task spawned 5 times (l8k2, kzqq, pg9l, cu0r, xy7n)
description:
**Problem:** The daemon spawned 'Extract spawn flags phase 1: --mode from cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go into pkg/orch/' five separate times as five separate beads issues: orch-go-l8k2, orch-go-kzqq, orch-go-pg9l, orch-go-cu0r, orch-go-xy7n. Each agent found the work was already done and produced no changes.

**Prior fix:** orch-go-dr0u fixed a single duplicate spawn (orch-go-09cc spawned twice). That fix clearly didn't hold — we're now at 5x duplication of the same task.

**Impact:** Wasted agent capacity (5 slots used for 1 task), verification backlog pollution (each dup needs completion review), and operator confusion. With max 5 concurrent agents, 5 duplicates means the entire capacity was consumed by one task.

**Likely cause:** The TTL-based dedup cache doesn't survive daemon restarts, or the cache key doesn't match across issue re-creation. Each duplicate has a slightly different beads ID but identical content.

**Expected:** Daemon should never spawn work that is already in-progress or completed by another agent. Dedup should check: (1) is an agent already working on this issue? (2) is identical work already done (same title/description)?

**Evidence:** Session on Feb 16 — closed l8k2, then kzqq appeared, then pg9l, then cu0r, then xy7n. All five confirmed work already done in commit 41f5a781.

title: Design session: collaborative tree-building as primary work interface
description:
Dylan imagines an interaction model where he and the AI orchestrator collaboratively build out the knowledge/work tree. In this model:

- The tree isnt two separate views (knowledge vs work) — its one living structure
- Nodes transform: issue (work) → completed → knowledge artifact (investigation, decision, model)
- Dylan and orchestrator grow branches together in conversation: 'this area needs a probe', 'this decision should spawn these three things'
- The tree IS the project. Issues are the mechanism, the tree is the interface.
- Lines between knowledge tree and work tree are blended as issues turn into knowledge

This is NOT the same as the knowledge tree visualization (orch-go-8f7). That is 'render what exists.' This is 'a new interaction model for shaping work.'

Key insight from session: in Dylans system, work and knowledge arent separate things. An investigation IS work that BECOMES knowledge. A decision IS knowledge that SPAWNS work. The current separation (beads for work, .kb for knowledge) is an implementation detail, not a real boundary.

Dylan said: 'this deserves its own design session.'

This is triage:review because it needs a real design session with Dylan present — this is about how HE wants to interact with the system, not something an agent can design alone.

title: Implement spawn-time accretion gate (block feature-impl on CRITICAL hotspots)
Architect: design language-agnostic accretion metrics for cross-project orchestration
description:
## Problem

Accretion gravity enforcement (file size thresholds, hotspot detection, completion gates) is currently hardcoded for orch-go — a Go project with specific structure. We want these metrics available for ANY project we orchestrate, regardless of language (Go, TypeScript, Python, Rust, Svelte, etc.) or file structure conventions.

## Key Design Questions

1. **Threshold calibration:** Are 800/1500 line thresholds universal, or do they need to be language-relative? (A 1500-line Python file is very different from a 1500-line Go file with verbose error handling. A 200-line Svelte component might be bloated while a 200-line Go file is fine.)

2. **What counts as a 'source file'?** Generated files (.svelte-kit/), test files (*_test.go), config files, vendored deps — how do we filter these language-agnostically?

3. **Project configuration:** How does a project declare its accretion policy? Per-repo config file? Convention-based defaults? Language auto-detection?

4. **Metric collection:** The accretion.delta event in orch complete uses git diff + wc -l — this is language-agnostic already. What else is needed?

5. **Integration with existing infrastructure:** orch hotspot, spawn gates, completion gates, coaching plugin — which parts are already portable vs orch-go specific?

## Evidence (orch-go stats as baseline)

Current orch-go file size distribution (490 source files, 154K total lines):
- 0-100 lines: 141 files (28.8%)
- 100-300 lines: 170 files (34.7%)  — median is 205 lines
- 300-500 lines: 93 files (19.0%)
- 500-800 lines: 42 files (8.6%)
- 800-1500 lines: 31 files (6.3%) — WARNING zone
- 1500+ lines: 13 files (2.7%) — CRITICAL zone

Top offenders: spawn_cmd.go (2173), doctor.go (1909), complete_cmd.go (1847), coaching.ts (1830), status_cmd.go (1625)

## Constraint

This is a design/architecture question. Produce recommendations, not implementation. Consider what a .orch/accretion.yaml or similar config might look like.

## Prior Knowledge

- Current enforcement: .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-inv-architect-design-accretion-gravity-enforcement.md (four-layer architecture, hardcoded 800/1500 thresholds)
- kn decision: Accretion gravity fix is structural extraction (strangler fig), not detection/warnings
- Accretion metric already captured: accretion.delta events in orch complete track file-level lines added/removed

description: Problem: AGENTS.md says 'Work is NOT complete until git push succeeds' but this is session-end guidance, not per-commit. Current orchestrator behavior is to push after every orch complete, which deploys partial infrastructure that can't function yet (e.g., pushing OauthToken model without ScsOauthClient that uses it). The right workflow is: build a coherent chain of changes, verify locally, push, verify in production. Need guidance in the orchestrator skill or AGENTS.md that distinguishes session-end push from mid-session commits that should batch before pushing. Solution: Add push strategy guidance — push after a coherent set of commits that can be verified end-to-end, not after every individual agent completion. Evidence: In price-watch, orchestrator repeatedly tried to push after completing pw-8d6u (OauthToken model) before the rest of the API collector chain was built. title: Verification gate inconsistency: spawn gate, daemon preview, and orch review use different definitions of 'unverified'
description:
**Problem:** Three code paths check for unverified work and produce different counts:

1. **orch review** — shows 0 pending completions (correct after clearing backlog)
2. **orch daemon preview** — showed 7 unverified (fixed by orch-go-6fer to filter closed issues in CountUnverifiedCompletions)
3. **orch spawn verification gate** — still shows 6 unverified and blocks spawns, even after the orch-go-6fer fix

The orch-go-6fer fix only patched one code path (daemon's CountUnverifiedCompletions). The spawn gate uses a separate check that wasn't updated. This is an architectural problem: multiple consumers of 'unverified work' state with no shared implementation.

**Impact:** Spawn gate blocks legitimate work. Only escape is --bypass-verification, which undermines the constraint. Observed today: couldn't spawn design session despite all reviewable work being completed.

**Expected:** Single source of truth for 'what work is unverified' that all consumers (spawn gate, daemon preview, orch review) use consistently.

**Evidence:** Session on Feb 16 — completed all items in orch review, spawn still blocked at 6. Had to bypass to spawn design session.

**Related:** orch-go-6fer (first instance fix, daemon preview only). Needs architect to review all verification state consumers and unify.

title: Create verification checkpoint file (~/.orch/verification-checkpoints.jsonl)
description:
Phase 1 of verifiability-first mechanical enforcement. The checkpoint file tracks which deliverables have been human-verified through both gates.

Schema per line: {beads_id, deliverable, gate1_complete (comprehension), gate2_complete (behavioral), timestamp, explain_text}

Integration points:
1. orch complete --explain 'text' writes checkpoint entry with gate1_complete=true
2. orch complete reads checkpoints — missing checkpoint for Tier 1 work = cannot close
3. Behavioral gate (gate2) tracked separately — feature/bug requires both, investigation requires only gate1

Currently: ~/.orch/verification-checkpoints.jsonl does not exist (ls returns 'No such file or directory').

The --explain flag already exists on orch complete (added by orch-go-w50) but does NOT write to a checkpoint file. It only stores as beads comment. Need the persistent checkpoint for daemon to read.

Related: .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md (Phase 1: Checkpoint Infrastructure)

title: Fix daemon completion processing to surface errors instead of silent continue
description: In cmd/orch/daemon.go:425-427, when CompletionOnce fails, the code logs an error but continues the daemon loop. This violates the spawn prerequisite fail-fast constraint (kb-035b64). title: Add accretion metric to orch complete: track file growth/shrinkage per agent
description:
## Goal

During orch complete, analyze the agent's git diff and log which files grew vs shrank. Over time this tells us whether accretion gravity is inverting.

## Metric to capture

For each file in the diff:
- file path
- lines added / lines removed
- net delta
- whether file is >800 lines (accretion risk zone)

Log to ~/.orch/events.jsonl as an accretion.delta event type.

## Future use

- Dashboard can show "lines added to files >800 lines" trend over time
- Alerts when monolithic files are growing despite extraction efforts
- Proves or disproves that strangler fig strategy is working

## Priority

P2 — not urgent, but the sooner we have data the sooner we can measure. Create after probe confirms the overall strategy works.

title: Audit: 4 verifiability-first issues closed without actual verification
description:
Four P1 issues from the verifiability-first decision were closed by agents that claimed Phase: Complete, but the work either didn't deploy or wasn't wired:

1. orch-go-6th (skill update): Agent updated source SKILL.md but never ran skillc deploy. Deployed skill has none of the changes. → New issue: orch-go-0psz
2. orch-go-7jl (daemon pause): Agent created VerificationTracker with passing tests but never wired it into daemon.go run loop. Dead code. → New issue: orch-go-ydzu  
3. orch-go-tyi (explain-back gate): Agent added the gate but it was later reworked by orch-go-w50. Need to verify the reworked version actually gates.
4. orch-go-w50 (rework explain-back): Agent claims --explain flag works. Flag exists but doesn't write to checkpoint file, just beads comment.

ROOT CAUSE: These were all completed by the previous orchestrator session which was itself violating verifiability-first (batch-completing without Gate 1 or Gate 2). The irony: the issues that implement verification were themselves never verified.

This is the pattern the decision warned about: 'Documentation without enforcement = data-plane artifact agents can ignore.' The agents produced locally-correct code (tests pass, build succeeds) that doesn't compose into a working system.

LESSON: Verifiability-first implementation issues must themselves be Tier 1 verified. Can't skip verification on the verification system.

Fix dashboard knowledge tree SSE cycling: disconnected state causes full re-render loop
description:
The /knowledge-tree dashboard page shows "disconnected" (red dot) for SSE and cycles through different tree configurations, resetting scroll position and expand/collapse state on each re-render.

SYMPTOMS:
1. Red dot "disconnected" in top right — SSE connection not establishing or repeatedly dropping
2. Tree visually cycles through different expand/collapse states
3. Scroll position resets on each cycle
4. User cannot browse the tree because it keeps re-rendering

ROOT CAUSE (likely):
SSE client is reconnecting in a loop. Each reconnection (or failed attempt) triggers a full tree re-render from /api/tree, which resets all UI state (expanded nodes, scroll position).

FIX APPROACH:
1. Fix SSE connection — why is it showing "disconnected"? Check /api/tree SSE endpoint, CORS, event format
2. Preserve expand/collapse state across re-renders — store in localStorage or component state, reapply after data update
3. Preserve scroll position across re-renders
4. Debounce SSE updates — dont re-render on every event, batch updates
5. Diff-based updates — only update changed nodes instead of full tree replacement

MINIMUM FIX: Even if SSE stays disconnected, the tree should render ONCE and stay stable. The cycling is the critical bug — SSE can be fixed separately.

VERIFICATION:
- Tree renders once and stays stable (no cycling)
- Expand/collapse state persists across data updates
- Scroll position preserved
- If SSE connects: live updates appear without resetting tree state
- If SSE disconnected: tree shows static snapshot, no cycling

title: orch clean can't purge ghost registry entries from derived state sources
description: Problem: After agents die (from orch complete Overmind cascade, missing projects, etc), orch status shows zombie entries indefinitely. orch clean handles disk state (OpenCode sessions, workspaces, investigation files) but the 4 derived state sources (OpenCode sessions, tmux windows, beads issues, workspaces) still produce ghost entries. Examples from today: pw-8966 (project not on disk), pw-8975, orch-go-y55 (already completed), untracked-Feb12-2144, untracked-Feb12-2219, untracked-Feb13-0933 — all showing as 'Active: 8' despite zero live agents. orch abandon fails on cross-project and untracked IDs. Solution: orch clean needs a --ghosts or --registry-purge flag that identifies entries with no live tmux window, no live OpenCode session, and no active beads issue, then removes them from the derived state. Or orch status needs to filter out entries that have no live backing source. Evidence: After running orch clean --all today, 8 ghost entries persisted. Zero tmux windows, zero live OpenCode sessions, but orch status still reports Active: 8.

title: Redesign work tab: grouping, sorting, and information hierarchy
description:
**Problem:** The work tab in knowledge-tree has multiple structural issues that make it hard to use:

1. **Related issues scattered** — dependency chains (e.g. verifiability-first Phase 1→2→3→4→5) are spread across the list instead of grouped. Issues sharing area: labels but without direct deps are also interleaved with unrelated work.

2. **In-progress items buried** — active work appears at arbitrary positions based on current sort, not surfaced to the top where it belongs.

3. **Decision/issue hierarchy inverted** — decisions appear as children of issues, but the real relationship is usually the reverse: decisions produce issues (decision → implementation issues). Current rendering implies issues are primary and decisions are subordinate.

4. **Sort logic unclear and insufficient** — no single dimension (priority, date, status, dependency) produces a useful ordering. Needs multi-dimensional: status urgency first, then grouping by dep chain / area label, then priority within groups.

5. **Verification gates invisible** — the verifiability-first constraint (Phase 3 session continuity gate) blocks spawns when unverified work exists, but this state is only visible when you hit the wall in the terminal. The dashboard should show: what's awaiting verification, what gates are blocking, and what action clears the block. Without this, the human can't understand or manage the system's constraints.

6. **Verification status not visible on issues** — completions awaiting review need to surface with tier and gate status (related: orch-go-aygz Phase 5).

**Desired outcome:** Work tab that groups related work together, surfaces active/blocked/awaiting-review items prominently, correctly represents the relationship between decisions and issues, and makes verification gate state visible so the human can manage the system proactively.

**Evidence:** Screenshot of current state shows verifiability-first phases scattered across 5+ screen positions despite being a single linear dependency chain. Orchestrator hit verification gate blind when trying to spawn — gate state was invisible until spawn failed.

title: Design solution for model artifact staleness — models drift from code

Examples: agent-lifecycle-state-model.md still referenced registry after pkg/registry/ was deleted (fixed manually during Feb 13 refactor). Models describe code paths, invariants, and constraints that become stale as implementation evolves. The probe system was designed to test model claims, but probes are manual and reactive — they don't catch drift from routine code changes. Need architectural decision on how to keep models accurate. Options might include: (1) code-change hooks that flag potentially-affected models, (2) model-code linkage metadata that enables staleness detection, (3) periodic model validation as part of completion workflow, (4) acceptance that models are approximate and only update during synthesis. Prior art: .kb/decisions/2026-01-12-models-as-understanding-artifacts.md, .kb/models/PHASE4_REVIEW.md, constraint 'D.E.K.N. Next field must be updated when marking Status: Complete'.

title: Knowledge-tree Work tab: hide or translate triage labels into user-meaningful status
description:
The Work tab shows raw 'triage:ready' and 'triage:review' labels on most issues. These are internal daemon workflow labels (controls whether daemon auto-spawns or orchestrator reviews first) and are meaningless to the user.

Options:
1. **Hide triage: labels entirely** — they're operational plumbing, not user-facing
2. **Translate** — triage:ready → 'queued', triage:review → 'needs review' (if status is useful)
3. **Filter to meaningful labels only** — show area:, effort:, priority but suppress workflow labels

Recommendation: option 1 (hide). The triage state is internal orchestration machinery. User cares about area, priority, and status — not daemon routing.

title: Test: dedup verification (delete after)
Knowledge-tree: intelligent sort instead of alphabetical
description:
Currently the Knowledge tab sorts clusters (folders) alphabetically, and nodes within clusters are also alphabetical. This is the least useful ordering — tells you nothing about importance, recency, or connectivity.

Better sort options:
- **Recency** (last modified) — recently touched knowledge floats to top. Probably best default.
- **Connectivity** (most links/references to other nodes) — central/hub knowledge ranks higher
- **Staleness** (oldest without updates) — surfaces decaying knowledge
- **Activity heat** (recent probe/investigation count) — shows active learning areas

Recommendation: default to recency sort, with a sort toggle allowing user to switch between recency/connectivity/alphabetical. Staleness could be a visual indicator (dimming) rather than a separate sort.

title: Surface verification tracker status in daemon bar and add gate banner
description:
## Context

The daemon's VerificationTracker pauses spawning when unverified completions exceed threshold (default 3). This state is only visible when a spawn fails in the terminal. The dashboard should show:
1. Current verification counter (e.g., '2/3 unverified')
2. Whether daemon is paused due to verification gate
3. Action hint ('Run orch complete to resume')

## Data source

Daemon status file (~/.orch/daemon-status.json) already has verification field:
```json
{
  "verification": {
    "is_paused": true,
    "completions_since_verification": 3,
    "threshold": 3,
    "remaining_before_pause": 0,
    "last_verification": "2026-02-15T..."
  }
}
```

But handleDaemon() in serve_system.go doesn't include this in the API response.

## Task

1. Add verification fields to DaemonAPIResponse in serve_system.go
2. Populate from status.Verification in handleDaemon()
3. Add verification fields to DaemonStatus interface in daemon.ts store
4. Display in daemon status bar: 'Verification: 2/3' or 'PAUSED (3/3)'
5. When paused, show amber/red banner below header (like focus breadcrumb)

## Files to modify
- cmd/orch/serve_system.go - add verification to DaemonAPIResponse + handleDaemon
- web/src/lib/stores/daemon.ts - add verification fields to DaemonStatus
- web/src/routes/work-graph/+page.svelte - add verification display in daemon bar + banner

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] /api/daemon response includes verification object when enabled
- [ ] Daemon bar shows verification counter
- [ ] Amber banner appears when daemon is paused for verification
- [ ] Banner includes action hint text
- [ ] go build ./cmd/orch/ succeeds

description:

FIX: In pkg/spawn/backends/headless.go, add time_ttl to the CreateSession call. Worker sessions should get a TTL (e.g., 4 hours after last update). Orchestrator sessions should get no TTL (persist until manually cleaned).

EXISTING CODE:

- OpenCode fork supports time_ttl field on session creation (commit f3c3865b8)
- Periodic cleanup job in OpenCode runs every 5 minutes, deletes expired sessions
- Respects active prompts — wont delete sessions with status = "busy"

SCOPE:
1. Add time_ttl to CreateSessionRequest in pkg/opencode/client.go
2. Set TTL at spawn time in headless.go (e.g., 4h for workers, none for orchestrators)
3. Verify OpenCode cleanup job picks up expired sessions

VERIFICATION:
- Spawned worker sessions have time_ttl set
- Sessions auto-expire after TTL (check OpenCode DB)
